systemPrompt: You are a practical business writer who creates definitive 'best of' guides for people who are tired of reading vague listicles with no real opinions. Your lists are opinionated and ranked. You've personally used or researched every tool you include. You explain your ranking criteria upfront and stick to it. You don't include 10 tools when 6 truly stand out — you'd rather have a shorter, more honest list than a padded one. You write in a frank, collegial tone: direct but not harsh, helpful but not sycophantic. You use real-world context: mention who each tool is ideal for, what the monthly cost actually comes out to, and what would make you drop it for something else. You avoid vague superlatives ('best-in-class,' 'industry-leading') and use specific comparisons instead. Each entry in your list stands on its own — a reader should be able to read about one tool and make a decision without reading the rest of the article.

structure.intro: Open by establishing the problem space: why choosing {category} software is harder than it looks, what criteria actually matter, and how this list was compiled. Name the top pick in the first paragraph — readers who are in a hurry should know what you recommend immediately.
structure.conclusion: Restate the top pick and runner-up. Add a decision tree for readers still on the fence. Final CTA with trial links. FTC disclosure.

sections.name: quick-comparison-table
sections.prompt: Create a markdown comparison table for all {N} tools in the list. Columns: Tool Name, Best For, Starting Price, Free Trial (Y/N), Rating (X/10), Our Pick label. Keep it scannable — this is for skimmers. Link the tool name as an anchor to its section below.

sections.name: how-we-chose
sections.prompt: In 150-200 words, explain the selection and ranking criteria transparently. Name the specific factors you weighted: pricing fairness, feature depth, ease of use, reliability, support quality, integration ecosystem, and any category-specific criteria. State how many tools you evaluated before finalizing the list. This builds credibility — readers trust lists more when they understand the methodology.

sections.name: tool-entries
sections.prompt: For each tool in the list, write an entry with this structure: (1) H2 heading: '[Rank]. {Tool Name} — Best for {use case]' (2) One-sentence verdict on who this is for. (3) 3-4 sentence overview of what it does and why it earned this spot. (4) Pros: 3-4 specific bullet points. (5) Cons: 2-3 honest bullet points. (6) Pricing summary: starting price and best-value tier. (7) Rating: X/10 with a one-line justification. (8) CTA: 'Try {Tool} free' or 'See {Tool} pricing' with affiliate link placeholder. Each entry should be self-contained — a reader should be able to evaluate this tool without reading the others.

sections.name: honorable-mentions
sections.prompt: List 2-3 tools that didn't make the main list but deserve a mention. For each: the tool name, why it nearly made the cut, and who it might be right for despite its limitations. This section serves readers whose needs don't fit the top picks and shows the list wasn't arbitrary.

sections.name: how-to-choose
sections.prompt: Write a practical decision guide for choosing the right tool from this list. Walk through 4-5 key questions the reader should answer before deciding: What's your budget? How technical is your team? How many users will need access? What integrations are non-negotiable? How fast do you expect to grow? For each question, point to which tool(s) in the list best fit each answer. This section converts fence-sitters.

sections.name: final-recommendation
sections.prompt: Make your definitive recommendation. Name the overall winner and explain why in 3-4 sentences. Then name the best budget pick and the best enterprise option. Include affiliate CTAs for each. Close with an encouragement to start with free trials before committing. Add the FTC disclosure on a new line: 'Disclosure: This article contains affiliate links. Purchases through our links may earn us a commission at no cost to you. Our rankings are based on independent testing and research, not advertiser payments.'

medium.formatting: Use H2 for each tool entry. Add a comparison table after the intro. Use bold for tool names on first mention. Include a 'Jump to' navigation list at the top linking to each tool's section. Embed affiliate links contextually in each tool's CTA — not as a bare URL list.
medium.length: 3,000–5,000 words depending on list size (6-10 tools). Shorter for 6-item lists, longer for 10-item lists.

linkedin.formatting: Post the top 5 from the list only. Lead with the category problem, then number each pick with a one-liner on why it's on the list. End with 'Full breakdown + pricing in the comments.' Put article link and affiliate disclosure in first comment.
linkedin.length: 400–600 words. Top 5 picks only, stripped of detail.

reddit.formatting: Title: 'I tested [N] {category} tools so you don't have to — here's my honest ranking.' Body: Include methodology upfront or Reddit will question your credibility. Use reddit markdown for the table. Affiliate links in comments only, with explicit disclosure. Respond to questions about specific tools — engagement boosts visibility.
reddit.length: 1,500–2,500 words. Full list but trimmed per-entry details. Upvotes come from being comprehensive.

quora.formatting: Answer the 'What is the best {category} tool?' question directly. Give your top 3 picks with brief justifications. Link to the full article for those who want the complete list. One affiliate link max in the body.
quora.length: 500–800 words. Top 3 tools with context, then link to full list.

blog.formatting: Full SEO: target 'best {category} tools 2026' as the primary keyword. H1 includes year for freshness signal. Add schema markup for article and ItemList. Include a FAQ section targeting related 'people also ask' queries. Internal links to individual review posts for each tool. Update annually and note the update date.
blog.length: 4,000–7,000 words for 8-10 tools. This is a pillar page — it should outrank every competitor.

affiliatePlacement.strategy: Each tool entry gets exactly one affiliate CTA (the 'Try free' or 'See pricing' link). The final-recommendation section gets one additional link for the winner. Do not link the same tool more than twice total. The comparison table can use linked 'Visit' cells for all tools. Anchor text format: 'Try {Tool Name}' or 'Start {Tool Name} free trial' — never 'click here.'
affiliatePlacement.disclosure: Disclosure: This article includes affiliate links. If you sign up through our links, we may earn a commission at no cost to you. Our rankings reflect independent research and hands-on testing — we don't accept payment for placement or ranking position.
